title: Small Channels Are Doing This Wrong | Here's the Fix

transcript.text: If you are struggling to grow your small YouTube channel, give us 15 minutes and we'll fix it for you. The channel direction is rock solid. >> And we just thought that looks excellent by itself. >> know about Impact font. It resembles a meme. >> This artificial look starts to lack authenticity for me. >> I like that they took the time to do their beauty shots, but >> We are going to break down a typical small YouTube channel. What they're doing right, what they're doing wrong, and the actionable steps all of you can take right now that will make the biggest difference to growing your channel. So, I'm here with Dan today. We spotted in our subreddit that Fast Freddy RC had submitted their thumbnail for auditing, and we were intrigued not just by the thumbnail, but by the entire channel because with 4,000 subscribers, 1,000 videos, but maybe they're not getting traction they want. Some of these videos are getting less than 100 views, which obviously for a creator might be quite demotivating at this point in their journey. So, we thought this would be a really good template channel to take a look at and share with all of you because there are many foundational things here that we think all creators could benefit from. And we're going to do this by taking a basic channel audit here, looking at four critical things. So, Dan, if I was to pitch this question to you to kick us off here, who is this channel for, and can we tell what the channel is about? >> Well, the name of the channel, like Fast Freddy RC, you immediately assume, you know, given the the artwork and stuff, oh, probably RC cars. And when you get into the videos and you look at the thumbnails and stuff, you were kind of like, oh, okay, art yeah, RC cars. Immediately, I know the channel is for enthusiasts, uh people who are probably very very deep into the RC car hobby. This isn't just the type of RC car you pick up at Walmart and you're playing with that day in the park. This is for folks who want to build the cars from scratch. >> We're looking at multiple step videos on how to build specific remote control cars. Thinking from a broader perspective, the accessibility of the channel, could that be a hindrance to you? >> Yeah, I I do think that it does make the content and the niche that they're in hyper-specific. The interest has to be because of the way the videos are constructed. It has to be one-to-one. The people watching this content pretty much are only going to watch if they also picked up the same exact kit and they are or they're getting ready to pick up that kit and they want to see how complicated it is, but they are ready to build that specific car. >> How many people in the entire world have bought this product? You may be thinking thousands, tens of thousands of people. And then, how many might be interested in like a a build with me piece of content? Now we're already down into like the hundreds or maybe the the dozens. >> At the same time, videos like this address a need that maybe is not being addressed by other creators. Imagine if this channel went away tomorrow and you the only reason you're into the hobby is because these videos exist and you're able to easily build along with this creator. I think with all of us things in mind, Dan, like we're now looking at the the first thing that we might do with this in terms of channel direction. Like, where might you be scoring this? I'd say out of 10 channel direction, I would say we're at like a nine out of 10. Like, the the channel direction is rock solid. >> But then, perhaps maybe that is a little bit too niche and it's really beneficial for a very small subset of viewers, but it means that it has a a very low ceiling in terms of views. And if I might pull it back down to like a seven? Do you want to Do you want to compromise on a seven? Yeah, we I I get what you're saying. All right. Next thing we want to do is target a specific video. And we're going to start with the title here. History of of unboxing the Tamiya Big Wig 2025 re-release. What's the bit and it's cut off because the title is too long, but to finish that title is what's the big deal. >> I don't really understand why the word history is in the title or the thumbnail. It's a 23-minute video. I assume there's going to be some history in the video already. The other The other thing that bothers me about the title is the the name brand. The The What's the Big Deal is being buried. >> come up with some uh potential suggestions here as well. Uh uh And one was the Tamiya Big Wig is back. But is it actually any good? So, we're taking out the the history and then the unboxing there by asking the curious question about whether it's going to be any good. Uh another example would be new Tamiya Big Wig release I've waited years. This is just conveying an excitement like I'm really keen to unbox this cuz I'm so passionate about it. And I thought another perspective here would be just the the the ultimate guide to the Tamiya Big Wig 2025. And what you're really selling there in the title is that this is comprehensive. What do we think about the original title? >> There's too much there. It's getting cut off and it's it is very matter-of-fact. It's just Yeah, you know, we're clocking in today and we're going to make a YouTube video. And that first title you have there, it But is it actually any good starts to speak to that opinion a little bit of like, "Eh, you know, what's the big deal?" If we're giving channel direction a seven, I kind of feel like titles should get like a five from me maybe. Like I think channel direction's rock solid. I think titles, all of the titles, I mean, we're just looking at one video, but all of the titles kind of have this issue where they're highly technical. Next thing obviously we need to do, Dan, is look at the thumbnail. What What What do we think just generally about this thumbnail? It's It's not bad. Uh I've I'm not a big fan of like green screen kind of backgrounds like that cuz it's just all those little lines and stuff are just adding some busyness that doesn't need to be there. Uh I don't know about Impact font. I I feel like And this is with the other thumbnails, too. I feel like that font choice is it resembles a meme. Yeah, and these are some of the other thumbnails where they're using that uh same font. But it's used I'm not going to say sparingly, it's used frequently in their thumbnails. So, there's there's so much to read at so many different places. This artificial look on the thumbnail, having this neon speedometer in the background, it'll it starts to lack authenticity for me. And we did some very quick mock-ups down here as alternatives in the RC niche. Just a box by itself on a table was effective. It's like really simple. Or if you were going to have yourself in a thumbnail, just something a little more authentic like you're at you're in a parking lot and you're about to test this. >> I completely agree. I think the title is doing a lot of work. If the thumbnail's job is to get you to stop scrolling and someone's holding up a thing in their thumbnail that you want, that you're thinking of getting for yourself, you're going to read the title. It looks like you you just walked out of the store and bought it and took a picture in the parking lot. I I think that that speaks volumes about who I'm going to be watching. >> And on the channel itself, there are a few examples where they do just have uh themselves in what looks to be their uh what what would I call this? Their RC man cave, the gar- garage. I'd call it studio, but >> [laughter] >> Okay, you you go with studio. But they are just like displaying the the built product and the box. And that again just feels more authentic and direct. And believe it or not, Dan, this one down here is a screenshot from the video itself. It's It's a screenshot from this thumbnail with the video. And we just thought that looked excellent by itself. Yeah, I think that that again, it adds the authenticity, but it also has all the stuff that they were already trying to put, right? We have We have them, we have their logo, and we have the thing that they're about to unbox. So, thinking about how we might score this, it's a serviceable thumbnail. They do have some text issues though, and almost by accident they've proved that they can make better thumbnails just from frames from videos. >> If I'm rating the titles out of five myself and kind of lumping the thumbnails in with the packaging, I think I'm all set to five with the current thumbnails. But we've already shown the potential for getting them from a five to I think a 10 like right away. >> All right. Now let's take a look at the video itself. Uh the titling and the thumbnail has convinced us to click. What is the big deal about the Big Wig 2025 re-release? Let's find out. >> [music] >> Welcome back to Fast Freddie RC, and today we're going to do an unboxing of the newly re-released re-release of the 2017 Tamiya Big Wig that was a re-release of the original Big Wig that came out in 1986. Goodness me, how many re-releases has there been? Now done, I was generous there. You know, YouTube typically says that the first 30 seconds of a video is the critical point of where a viewer stays or goes. We're now at 52 seconds because we had to get through uh quite a montage of literally the box. I liked that they took the time to do their beauty shots of the box, but I would have brought in them talking a lot sooner if you wanted to use that B-roll up front. >> You can combine this A-roll and this B-roll together so that you first of all get some face-to-face time with the viewer in the first four or five seconds and then and then you jump to that B-roll. I'd love to get to know Freddy a bit quicker than the the 30-second montage, yeah. Now, just before we kind of rate this I did want to show you that in other videos, it's actually quite the opposite in the sense that if we just jump into one of these videos where they're looking at building one of the models, like let's do step 12 here. So, for step 12, attaching the rear axles, well, you can see here that we're going to be greasing up our drive shafts. One end's going to go into our gearbox, one end's going to go into the axle. >> You're just thrown immediately into step 12. There's no reference to we're building this particular model. This is how it's going to look like at the end. In one video it took too long to get to delivering the value and then in this one, the viewer has no parachute. They're just they're dropped immediately into whatever this is and I might be overwhelmed by that. >> When we saw them doing an unboxing and introducing themselves to what I assume was a larger audience than they're used to, I am going to say that that wasn't bad. I I would rate that at like a seven right now. I I appreciated the effort that they put into it and I think literally in their video editing software, it would have just been one easy tweak of taking the sound that was about to be played and just putting it underneath the B-roll and that would have been like a perfect intro. Some of these hooks intros just force you into something midway through a conversation or a build. It's like sometimes I upload these really self-contained videos for for anybody to kind of jump into the hobby with me and then I get back to uploading these really long series and the series aren't in order. And so if I'm subscribing, I'm not getting that satisfying conclusion or satisfying next step in the next video. And as I'm scrolling through the most popular videos, these all seem to be the stand-alone videos that are performing a lot better because again it has that broader audience reach. Something that they could try done, this is done in a gaming space quite a lot, is they could take one of their multi-part build videos that they already have on the channel, put everything together in a single video and upload it and just see how how it performs. >> Yes, I I think if you do this hobby and an 8-hour video pops up of a build, and if that's normal, if that's the amount of time it typically takes, talk about an authentic experience. You're like, "Oh, that's exactly what I need. This creator's listening and wants to try this." All they have to do is take the videos, take one playlist, take a popular one, and turn it into one video. And I promise you, uh you know, the results from that over time will be better than all of the individual videos combined because it's so much easier to navigate one video versus a playlist of videos that were all uploaded across different times. The other thing I was going to say is I kind of feel like they're uploading on a content schedule that they've given themselves, and it's causing them to do the strategy of breaking up their content, which I don't think is necessary. >> Looks to be four videos a week, I would say done. Uh potentially more. I think think a channel like this could get away with uploading a couple times a month if they're going to do these longer videos where they're building a whole thing. How do we bump up this channel's score so the next time we look at them, we're giving them nines and 10s? You're going to need some more content buckets I think to bring in new viewers. So my suggestions were once in a while, even just once ever, show off your collection. And then maybe some videos on snowmobiles talking about getting into this hobby itself. Videos that talk about the price to get into it, a good beginner car, all of that stuff, the beginner level stuff I think would take your channel and introduce to so many new people. Titles are functional. They have the SEO in there. The challenge was that they were somewhat functional, but it lacked maybe the intrigue or the curiosity that's going to convince somebody to click. That sentiment also extends to the thumbnails in terms of text on the thumbnails. There is so much of it and it often repeats what the title says that it's kind of redundant. And we felt that a more authentic organic image with maybe you in your let's call it a studio with the product might have more appeal to it. >> The intros are high effort right now at least in those videos where you're unboxing stuff. I like them. Currently with the intros we have they're not getting introduced to it in a way that I think makes it less intimidating or or feel welcoming. It's more like, oh this is kind of cool, but what is it? What like what am I watching still? If you want to get your own mini channel audit right now, then you can point your browser at vidIQ.com/youtube-channel-audit and it will give you a quick summary of where you are as a creator on YouTube. We did this for Fast Freddy RC and the numbers are looking fairly solid except for the views to subscribe ratio cuz we see that they've got 4,000 subscribers, but view counts are often 100 or less which kind of leads to what we've been suggesting about maybe unapproachable content or packaging that needs some work. And if you enjoy this conversation about YouTube channels, then believe it or not we do this every single week on a Tuesday when we live stream auditing your channels. And if you want to see the latest one, it's over here right now. Thanks for watching. We'll see you again soon.
